Reviews for CMSC216

Information Review
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Anonymous
02/26/2026 		Was falsely accused by Kauffman last semester. Completely wrecked my relationship with UMD CS imo. He published his own statistics on AI cheating in his class which showed that at least 19.6% of the class was penalized with a grade reduction or a pending XF case (XF is an academic integrity mark which removes CS students from the major.) Even more students than that were accused. On one project, he mass accused and penalized 15% of the class for cheating using compiler-generated code or AI because they used an "advanced" command, even though it was listed in the class slides, project rubric, project description, textbook, and an approved linked webpage on his course site. He also said we could reference compiler-generated code for the project and even bring AI-generated notes and content to exams, which makes it really unclear about what’s actually allowed. He originally accused even more students for the same issue until realizing he had accidentally included a non-project-related file in his checker. He said he had high confidence in the accusation, and offered a 0 to those accused on the project (~2% overall grade reduction) or a one-on-one interview process where success would lead to a regrade request and failure results in a XF recommendation to OSC. Over 1/3 of the accused students still showed up despite the existential threat of taking the interview, which makes me believe that he was really throwing around accusations. Either way, Kauffman later wrote in a post that he "was never once convinced of a student's innocence during an interview" and those who were let go only had "plausible deniability." The process was extremely stressful and lacked transparency. At the end of his course, 20 students in his section went to OSC. For context, there were 50 AI-related OSC across all departments and classes at UMD for the entire year of 2023, meaning his section last semester would account for around half of that. Whether you believe it or not, I know many people who were accused, didn't cheat, and were penalized anyway, including myself. His passive aggressive comments about not giving extra credit on later projects because of the cheating cases and verbal/written rants about "cheating" students devaluing the CS degree before any of the cases were even resolved made the class feel toxic, distrustful, and stressful like everyone was constantly under suspicion. On top of the difficult content, the threat of being accused and kicked out of the major over one or two lines of code on a project that is flagged as suspicious could seriously impact anyone’s mental health. He may be allowing AI in future courses to avoid this issue again (or in his words, because too many people are "cheating"), but this is my two cents. 2 stars because I learned a lot in his class as mentioned by everyone else below me.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A+
Anonymous
01/23/2026 		intro: insanely passionate professor that is always working on the class and how it works, pretty cool in general pros: - open note exams - fun lecturer that records lectures (i mainly had to watch but he is good) - good on answering questions, being available to students, even goes on piazza every now and then - gradescope homeworks that are quizzes, can retake - labs are useful to content - projects are fair, mostly public tests then manual inspection - extra credit available based on class engagement and some other small factors which helps with grades - constantly looks for feedback through surveys - piazza is good, incentive to discuss and engage with each other, prof will be on there at times - besides recordings/slides/example code, other things are also posted and are available for use - practice exam reviews a bit of the content, it is reviewed in lecture before exam (good overall) - makeup credit available on projects in a way - TAs are pretty helpful neutral - flow of class/content/lectures expects you to be on top of work constantly rather than doing it last minute including him asking questions in lecture to class - "required" textbook, available online when I took class, reading it is not actually necessary but it seems like very good supplemental material - discussion section not necessary to attend (when I took class) but offers good reinforcement on labs and content cons - one part of labs is a quiz section but you don't know what questions are right or wrong when you check the answers, so sometimes its hard to know what you did wrong and end up wasting extra time conclusion: take the class! he's a good professor
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting a B+

01/20/2026 		Amazing professor! All lectures are recorded, exams are graded fairly, labs encourage group work and actual learning. Rewards effort in the class through small extra credit/make up credit which can help your grade if you're 1 point away from the next letter. I would highly recommend him.

CMSC216

Expecting an A+
Anonymous
01/05/2026 		Great professor. While he can be a bit critical and passive aggressive, he does an outstanding job at actually teaching the course content. Each topic is explained in great detail, there is a ton of resources provided outside of lecture to study and review. The course itself is also quite fun, but can be challenging. Also open note midterms and final is pretty great. Highly recommend taking him whenever possible.
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Expecting a C
Anonymous
12/29/2025 		I'd like to start off by saying that he is not inherently the worst of the worst, I think that his approach at teaching is not the best, but I don't think he means it with ill-intent. I barely scraped by and ended with a 73% which resulted in a C, even after always scoring below the average on all his exams and quizzes. The way that the course was structured, at least when I took his class, was set up in a way that the projects and exercises made up a substantial part of the overall course grade. Lab works also made up a small but considerable percentage that can boost your grade by the time finals come around. If I am not mistaken at the beginning of the course, he acknowledged that his exams and quizzes were known to be difficult, but that as long as you completed the projects and exercises on time and tried your best in exams and quizzes you were going to be okay. Projects: His projects were not inherently difficult, they were at times confusing, but attending office hours and posting on Piazza ultimately cleared any confusions you may have had. Exercises could often be done in a day, but I would suggest not doing this for both exercises and projects. Lab works were also easy to complete, and we were given enough time to complete them. Just by completing these on time, you can be at a good standing before exams and quizzes tank you grade (and they will, unless you are the type to ruin the curve.) Exams/Quizzes: His exams and quizzes were confusing, tedious, and soul draining. Often times it was never truly clear what exactly we were going to be tested on. Before exams or quizzes, he would send out emails containing a list of what we had covered since the previous quiz/exam, and he would say that what was on the slides was testable material, so it would be hard to differentiate what exactly was going to be on the test/quiz. On one occasion, what he stated was going to be covered on a quiz were assembly function calls and parent-child processes; given the difficulty and notoriety of his quizzes I studied everything assembly and assembly-function call related, and only studied parent-child processes some half hour before the quiz and ultimately the quiz only contained one assembly multiple choice question and the rest of the quiz was all related to parent-child processes; this time the quiz was easier than expected, but the angst and confusion leading up to the quiz takes a toll on you. Part of the shortcomings related to quizzes and exams *could* be attributed to a student's lack of complete familiarity and understanding of the material, however, unless the only thing you have going for you is school (and that is okay, great actually, if it's the case for you - it is not often, rarely actually, for that to be the case for everyone) then it is very difficult to develop mastery of the material in time before exams and quizzes. Some topics may be covered a lecture or two before an exam/quiz and some of the questions on quizzes and exams feel like they are meant to be 'gotchas' or may be super specific to a covered topic. For example, though the understanding of unix commands is useful in various industries, some questions often relied on a lab discussion slide that may have shown some [blank] unix command that was only used then and never really again - to some students those questions may have felt like free points, but when you are handling other courses and only have a vague idea of what was to be on a quiz then, some miscellaneous unix command would feel like the least of your concerns. I personally scored poorly on exams and quizzes, at some point getting a 39% on an exam, fully tanking my grade and putting my chances to pass on the line. However, because I submitted all other projects, exercises and labs on time, my grade was brought up just enough to pass. Yoon as a person: Results may vary, but Yoon was extremely approachable and a kind understanding person. If you come to his office hours or email him, he is very helpful and he often responds to emails fast. I often stayed after class to ask clarifications or questions related to the content, so did others, and he would make sure to explain and breakdown your confusions so as to help you better understand the material. Moreover, if you maintain a clear communication with him about certain issues and difficulties, he is often willing to help and possibly grant small extensions for submissions. He is also kind of funny when you talk to him one on one. I would say, do not walk into his class seeing him as some sort of evil supervillain who sits in his office petting a cat while plotting on how to make his next exam as hard as possible. If I am not mistaken, being a professor is not his main role at the university, take a look at his extensive resume and his achievements and you sort of realize that being a professor is just a role he has to play, and he can only have so much time on his hands, so balancing all of these responsibilities may be difficult even for him. p.s. I say all this not to glaze him or hype him up, all I'm saying is that it's probably a lot of work to handle but he's kinda goated if you talk to him - ppl like to make him seem like the devil himself but he's not too bad. Yoon as a professor: Now, regarding his teaching style, it is not really the best. Attend all his lectures and you will realize that not many people show up. He likes to go into a lot of specific niche topics while teaching, and he likes to draw on his iPad to breakdown what may be happening regarding memory allocation and a lot of other visual stuff. Often times this gets confusing, as he draws on top of the slides, so it is not visually the best and easiest thing to understand unless you follow along closely. If you walk into class during these moments when he draws out topics, it looks like that one picture 'my coworker losing his damn mind.' Moreover, sometimes he goes back and forth with a lot of coding examples and slides, so it gets a little confusing to understand. It is hard to keep up sometimes and it would often require revisiting his recorded lectures to get a better grasp of a concept. On the times he does do well, then it is easy to follow along and he does a good job at walking you through some of the topics. Class logistics: Honestly not the best at all. This semester, we only found out about our exam results a couple of days before the final. Some projects take a long time to get graded, and the late submission turns on after 11 pm of the on-time submission date. Some lab works were graded the day of grade submissions, so it was difficult to truly get what your grade was throughout the semester. Honestly, it was gut wrenching not knowing where you stood before the final exam was added to the grade book. He would often curve, at some point in some semester he curved by 8% percent, but this semester he did not curve as much, only slightly so that was also gut wrenching. TLDR: Not the worst. His exams and quizzes are hard and may tank your grade, but the class is set up in a way where your grade is able to take it if you do all the other work on time. As a person he's chill, as a professor he struggles, your grade will never be truly clear until the day grades are due. If you have to take him because every other section is closed prepare for a rough semester but if you show up and do your best you should be good. Not truly the end of the world, but you will have to put your head down to make it through.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

12/28/2025 		He teaches pretty well, you come out of the class with a good understanding of 216 content. The worst part about this class are the projects, they're long and hard, start early. Expecting an A-
Anonymous
12/28/2025 		All of the projects, homeworks, labs are very useful and relevant for the exams. Nothing in this class is that difficult, but make sure to start on all projects as soon as possible. Kauffman is always heavy on catching cheating on the projects; just give them your best honest attempt and I promise you'll be fine. His slides are very wordy and often contain a lot of useless information, and you're meant to rely more on your own work for the exams (they are open resource). Kauffman is by far the best choice for 216 right now and it's not really close.

Expecting an A+
Anonymous
12/27/2025 		A phenomenal amazing passionate considerate professor. Kauffman provides you with all the resources you need to succeed. He clearly cares a lot. - incredibly fair exams (open note too) - detailed and well-structured projects - very quick Piazza responses - lecture and lab/project walkthrough videos on Panopto - leniency with some late projects - so many opportunities for bonus points / extra credit / ways to save your grade I’d recommend him 1000x

CMSC216

Expecting an A-
Anonymous
12/24/2025 		Amazing Professor! Lectures are very engaging and he explains things clearly. Grading is very fair and he gives lots of opportunities for bonus/make up credit. Labs and HWs are relatively easy and focus on introducing concepts. Projects are hard but not impossible and they truly teach you the material. Exams are fairly difficult but they do reflect what's taught in class and are similar to the practice exams given. If you study hard and understand the labs and projects, you can definitely do well on the exams. Re doing labs on paper or parts of projects is a great way to study, the exams usually have pretty normal coding questions with the rest being short answer. The short answer matters just as much as the coding in terms of grading which isn't bad by any means. He gives extra credit for participating in lectures and answering questions on piazza which is a great idea!! I initially was motivated to participate for the extra credit but after the first few weeks of class, I found myself participating out of genuine curiosity or just wanting to engage with the material. For piazza, this was the one class where students would always respond to questions quickly which was very helpful. Overall, Kauffman is a great professor and I'm glad I got to take this class with him!
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting a B+
Anonymous
12/21/2025 		There's a lot of things that Kauffman does well like the format of the exams (open note, open terminal) and the instructiveness of the projects (the project descriptions are very thorough). He also records lectures and really tries to make sure that you can do well in the class. However, the main reason I took off 3 stars is because of all the AI accusations he was throwing around. Like I was not accused, but I know so many people who were falsely accused of using AI and that literally made it so scary just to go to class. Even though I wasn't doing anything wrong, I always felt like I would be falsely accused. I think that this part of the course needs to change because while it is important to ensure that the class is fair for students who actually do their work, I think that before Kauffman goes around accusing people, he needs to be 100% sure they actually did something wrong.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A+
Anonymous
12/21/2025 		Kauffman is an incredible professor and 216 is pretty easy with him. His lectures are incredibly detailed and informative. The structure of the class is very well made and makes it easy to understand everything. Every assignment given gives enough practice for the exams (you should not chatgpt the projects for this reason) so you don't need to practice much outside of the coursework and if you didn't understand something in lecture the labs and projects will usually fill these gaps. The projects only have public tests and the exams are open laptop so you can download anything you want for the exam just no google or ai. One warning I'll give is he told us he would reorganize the grading structure to deal with ai cheating and his options were either lower project weighting and raise exam weighting, add an extra midterm, or automatically fail anyone who's exam average is below 70%. Personally I don't think these should be an issue due to what I've written in this review. If you're reading this and picking classes definitely take this guy over anyone else
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Anonymous
12/16/2025 		Genuinely horific class. There's a ton of reviews that elaborate on why he's so terrible but it really does not compare to how awful it is to actually be in this class. Please, PLEASE do not take Yoon if you can help it. Hell, even if you would have to delay taking this class, that might be preferable if you're already struggling mentally. I mean this seriously. Projects are poorly documented and extremely tedious. Classes are unbelievably boring and nigh unfollowable with how poorly Yoon can actually qualify what he is teaching. Quizzes and exams consist of either extremely tedious coding, very niche knowledge, and the memorization that'll have you wondering whether anything you learn in this class will actually stick with you: it won't. On multiple ocassions you will think this class has drained your passion for CS. I can know state with certain that I still like CS, but that Yoon has doon irreperable damage to my view of UMD and any form of education it could teach me in the subjects that actually matter to me. If this isn't enough to convince you, Kaufman apparently has open notes. Avoid Yoon at all cost.
Nicole Kyei-Asare

Expecting an A
Anonymous
12/09/2025 		I genuinely like Yoon as a professor and of the CS professors I've had at UMD so far, Yoon is my favorite. Though it's obvious I'm odd in that. He's really nice and will happily answer any questions you have, and its very clear he cares about teaching the class and students success. I don't think there's anything that feels unfair at any point, and he often puts up clarifications (or honestly borderline hints) for exams/quizzes part way through for common clarifications. I think the projects could have clearer explanations on the assignment docs, but I think some of that comes down to real-world edge cases or behavior vs what they want to and can test in a class project sometimes not lining up exactly. I will say, the TAs were very hit or miss this semester and any real complaints I do have about the course come down to that, since the grading can take a while and some of the responses on piazza were not the best.
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Expecting a D+
Anonymous
10/23/2025 		If you haven't already, go look at that rating and the amount of reviews. On god it is not a lie, he deserves it. When I tell you his class will be a detriment to your mental health and academic career I'm not joking. I'm going break down why though: 1. His teaching style is to essentially overload you with a bunch of information, most of which isn't relevant for the upcoming task such as project or test/quizzes. So while you're busy trying to sift through what you need and don't, along with trying to understand the topic at hand, he's already moved on to the next. Which is infuriating because I'm paying thousands of dollars for this to be my experience. 2. Remember what I said about this unnecessary things he'll teach in class? Yeah he'll make those small things important questions on his test/quizzes. 3. He will, and I mean will, overload you with work. The best way to put it, he operates as if his class is the only one you're taking. To give you an example, literally the night of our first exam, he assigned the next coding project and told us "I suggest you get started on this immediately". I got the notification for the assignment literally late night while me and a friend were studying for HIS exam that was going to happen later that day in Mckeldin. As you can image we were pissed. And he also gives little-to-no time between projects to breath. 4. Speaking of the projects, his project documents are longwinded and vague. So much so that the piazza is littered with people asking for clarification on things that should be crystal clear constantly. And you can get a bunch of points docked if you miss just 1 of the several details he gives you in his, on average, 7 page docs. Which if you ask me, it makes no sense that you have a doc that long and people in droves are still asking for basic clarification. You wrote all that for nothing then. 5. The TAs are hit or miss, and it's almost dramatic. Some are really cool and helpful, while others feel as if they think just being there while offering little to no should guarantee them a check. I had to hassle one of them to tell me what was wrong with my code for like an hour as I obviously didn't know and tried almost everything at that point. And once they finally helped me, it took them like 10 minutes to find the issue and relay it to me. There has also been multiply times where TAs will change their hours without updating the google calendar, and as a commuter it's just rage inducing. And I've brought this to Yoon's attention before and he acts as if it's a trivial matter. Like bro I'm wasting gas and time and he's so tight with his deadlines it makes no sense that he's chill about this. (Now the TA thing isn't that important since they're subject to change, but considering he chooses who becomes his TA, I think it's still worth mentioning). 6. His late policy. I don't think it's bad but I think it's worth mentioning and from here you can decide whether you're cool with it. So essentially you can turn in a assignment late only 24 hours after it's initial due date. So if it wad due the 21st at 11pm (yes all his projects are due at 11 pm, not 11:59pm), then the late due date is the 22nd at 11pm. And he will reduce the overall grade for this assignment by 15%. But don't get this confused with the GFA requirements, where if you don't meet them for each project you will be dropped by a letter grade. You can go back for any project to meet the GFA requirements (which could be to just pass all public test for example), but you won't get a new grade for that assignment. So if you get a 75% on that project, that's what you're stuck with. In all, please just avoid his class. It's unnecessary stress and I'd recommend that you don't pair this class with cmsc250. Take one or the other, especially if you can't escape his class for whatever reason (like me). And sure he has a seemingly nice personality and all, but if he was truly as nice as people say I don't think he'd work his students to the bone the way he does. It's needless and truly from my experience, I won't be able to say with confidence that I know how to code C by the end of this class since I and many others just understand enough to get the projects out of our faces and to get barely passing grades on his quizzes and exams. So if you want to actually know what you're doing, don't take him. GOOD LUCK!
Larry Herman
CMSC216

Expecting an A

Expecting an A
Anonymous
10/15/2025 		I want to say: I've gotten 90-100% on everything so far, this isn't a grade thing. The only avg I can see for quizzes so far is quiz1, which is ~20/30 so right in line with what people say of "most people technically fail every assessment.", so take my own grade with a grain of short term salt. I hate writing reviews mid-semester for classes since I think it can come off as salty, but given he's teaching the class again in Spring I must do my part for students registering for classes lol. Yoon is actually a nice person. BUT. The class quality is genuinely atrocious, I wanted to disagree with the previous reviews but really the fact that this class is costing me money is rather depressing. I don't personally have any issue with the lectures but I also don't pay any attention during them, since skimming the slides is usually plenty, but that really doesn't excuse the teaching quality. The projects aren't that bad, as long as you sit down and do them with a couple days of time to work since they can involve a lot of writing code even if its straightforward. My only issue with the projects is the same issue I have with quizzes and assume I will have with the exams: the requirements (or on assessments the phrasing of questions) is usually so poor that they simply don't make sense or are missing key details for you to do the work. It's been the case on every project so far that there is an absolute deluge of posts asking "what does this actually mean" about project specs, because the phrasing of a spec will be vague enough that you could interpret it many different ways and the public tests will be so incredibly simple that they don't address a single one of the clarifications needed. There's also often not actually any details given on expected formatting for things that require formatting, and TAs/instructor will sometimes give conflicting answers with each other to questions. Whether I end this class with a C- or an A+ I'll leave having learned the exact same amount from the lectures and projects: absolutely nothing. The fact UMD is ranked as high as it is for CS and then has professors for lower level classes that cannot teach and TAs which vary from "make the class easy as pie" to "Lecture round 2 with no benefit to the students at all" is rather insane. You would think it in the school's interest to attempt to give students a strong foundation so they can confidently get internships, do research, do well in later classes, but I'm pretty sure the avg student coming out of this class will only have gained the knowledge that "they don't like C/assembly" based on a bad prof.
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Expecting a D+
Anonymous
09/03/2025 		Oh Yoon, Oh Yoon, where do I start? This was single-handedly the most BEAUTIFUL class I've ever taken during my long, long, 7 years at College Park, Maryland. Yoon explains things so beautifully even a monkey could understand assembly and linux. I'm convinced the 85% of the class that gets below an A has an iq between 41 and 67. I showed my pet goldfish the Yoon lectures (he didn't record them, so I uploaded them myself using my neuralink) and he showed up to the lecture and got a 97 on the final. He invoked such an incredible passion for low-level programming in me I decided to skip the entirety of my undergrad and do a PHD at Tsinghua in compilers. I came to class every day, and he handed me extra points for simply existing, because he's such a beautiful soul. GOON WITH YOON! #YOONERS
Nelson Padua-Perez

Expecting an A+
Anonymous
06/20/2025 		Really well ran course. The projects were pretty instructive, especially the Assembly project. Considering that the tests are borderline open internet, they're surprisingly easy, and if you can do the projects, you can definitely do well on the exams. There's so many places to get bonus points, so even if you lose some points here or there, you can get them back really easily. The lectures can be a bit slow at times, but physical attendance isn't mandatory so if that's an issue you can just watch the lecture videos at your own pace. He loves answering questions, so if you're struggling with a concept, he'll make sure you understand it. Taking 216 with Kauffman really is a no-brainer, just do it.
Nelson Padua-Perez

Expecting a C+
Anonymous
06/06/2025 		pros: smart dude, good demonstrations, worksheets,projects,participation is grade padding, and final exam questions are very similar to the midterm and practice exams. cons: no phones no laptops during lecture, he wastes probably 5 minutes each class stuttering or calling people out for using their phone. unforgiving n strict, slow at grading, he gatekeeps online communication and office hours behind a fat block of text and rules u gotta read (this one not that bad just annoying). multiple choice section on exams is notoriously difficult and specific, he also DOCKS POINTS FOR WRONG ANSWERS which is apparently to "discourage guessing" but inherently makes every second guess themself lol. we had a failing midterm average. lectures are also incredibly boring but thats expected for any cs class ig. he did curve final grades idk what the dude 2 below is sayin looking back i would take an 8am to avoid larry in a heartbeat but thats jus me

CMSC216

Expecting an A-
Anonymous
06/02/2025 		One of the best professors I have ever had. I think he truly structures his course through a student's perspective. The fact that he records his lectures and lab walkthroughs really allowed me to fully understand the course concepts. I feel like he gave adequate practice materials for exams and although they were REALLY long, his projects were one of the biggest reasons I did well in this course. His long and intensive projects covered basically everything that we would need to know for the course and doing the projects helped me not only understand the topics conceptually but also in practice. My advice to future students would be to really understand the projects because thats usually what the coding question for exams will be based off of.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A+
Anonymous
05/29/2025 		One of the best professors I've had yet. I thought his lectures were well-paced, informative and easy to understand. He is an engaging and personable lecturer who takes time to answer questions and his participation system provides a nice padding to your overall grade if you happen to need that. The later projects were ROUGH but not impossibly difficult with a reasonable amount of effort, starting on them early is definitely for the best. His open note exams (which I greatly preferred) test your ability to apply concepts instead of memorize random details, for obvious reasons. I def think your grade reflects the amount of effort you put in, and honestly this class was much less stressful than 132 for me.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A
Anonymous
05/29/2025 		Great professor who cares about his students' learning. Great class structure with lots of flexibility, difficult but rewarding projects, and easy but not free exams. You get what you put into this class.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A
Anonymous
05/29/2025 		This class wasn't too bad, but it really did not feel as rewarding as how the other reviews said. The structure of the class was split upon 5 projects, 2 exams and a final, hw "quizzes" (they're incredibly easy), and labs. The projects were quite difficult in my opinion (3 and 4 were the worst but in different ways I'll explain later), but they were good practice. PLEASE DO NOT GPT THEM, AND PLEASE START THEM EARLY (trust me you will thank me). They are designed in a way that is quite different from 132, they are very long and test the information covered very thoroughly, but it is good preparation for the exams. I thought projects 1, 2, and 5 were relatively straight forward with a decent difficulty, project 3 was pretty difficult because Assembly is just like that, but project 4 was really hard because it was assigned late (giving us less time to do it), but it was an added difficulty to figure out which tests were actually passing because it would work half of the time due to the concurrency (sometimes, tests would pass on grace, but completely fail on gradescope). The exams were actually not that bad, they tested content covered in the most recent project to the date of the exam, and were open note. But I think the open note concept is kind of misleading, it doesn't make the exam easier, it just made it a different kind of test, covering applications of topics rather than fundamentals which you may be used to from previous courses. The labs and hw were good practice if you diligently did them, and were overall pretty free grades. Kauffman did pretty unique system for participation called engagement points, which was counted under the labs you completed, but any extra lecture participation or piazza participation would be counted as bonus, which was pretty nice. The content covered wasn't really too difficult in my opinion, but I felt lectures were pretty pointless. His slides I felt made the content way more complicated than it needed to be, and his speaking was way too technical and not easily digestible. During discussion, a lot of times it felt the TAs couldn't really give me in depth answers for questions, and mainly just went over the labs. As a result, most of the time I would learn either reading the textbook or yt vids. A lot of times too, the way Kauffman focused on materials was more related to computer hardware than the coding, which made it feel more applicable for Computer Engineering majors, and I felt like the mateiral I was learning was kinda useless compared to how other teachers taught it. Some of the reviews talked about him being condescending, others talked about him being super nice, I thought he was fine overall in approachability, but like none of the extremes. TL;DR: Kauffman is a fine professor, but I felt a lot of the reviews are rating him higher than he should be. The course content isn't too difficult, but sometimes it was pretty frustrating using the terminal and grace, and its just overall a very different experience from previous courses. Once again DO NOT GPT PROJECTS, START THEM ON DAY 1 - you will suffer the consequences otherwise trust me.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting a C
Anonymous
05/24/2025 		Even though I struggled tremendously in this course, Kauffman seems to care about his students' success. He is especially engaging in lectures and extremely helpful during office hours and the staff are always there to help on OH, Piazza, and Discussion. I believe that if you put in the effort and dedication in this course, you will be able to do well. I will say, though, that you should start ALL of Kauffman's projects on day 1 because these projects are not easy, and they require a lot of focus and attention, and you will have to know the content very well. In addition, his exams are not easy, so make sure you really understand your notes, slides, projects, lecture code, etc. I will give him 3 stars, however, because he does make this class somewhat stressful, but I do think that these stressful experiences will continue in later CMSC classes (not to say that they're bad, but they will become more rigorous, and students will need to work harder to do well). Kauffman provides tremendous grade padding from manual inspection on projects and a fair engagement point system, which can tremendously help students improve their grades. If you are someone who is interested in low-level systems, then I believe that you should definitely take 216 with Kauffman, and you will like it !
Larry Herman
CMSC216

05/24/2025 		as someone with 9 years of programming experience here are the pros and cons of larry the lecturer not professor: tldr: will you fail? only if you dont try your best pros: - practice exams cover most relevant material (enough to get an A) - he will actually give you all the material/instructions - he will repeat material that will be on a test multiple times in a row during lecture - all of the material is important (some people were falling asleep, this is the basics of computers, get used to it) - wont argue about ADS accommodations (just visit his office hours) cons: - annoying policies (not talking about attending class or electronics, that is normal. youre paying for the lecture hall, go to it??) - teaches some content only from you reading the textbook (only in 216) - slow at grading, not the TAs fault, hes just slow - wont let you email him (at least hes nice in office hours) - rambles a bit

Expecting an A
Anonymous
05/23/2025 		Kauffman is so goated and beloved by all his students. He made content that I otherwise would've found relatively uninteresting (operating systems, unix processes, assembly) engaging. He is incredibly passionate about teaching and always takes time to answer questions during lecture, and also post lecture recordings as well as lab overviews. I learned so much from him this semester and you can tell he actually cares about making sure his students understand content. The projects can be difficult, and I'd recommend starting them early, as well as using Piazza as much as you can because office hours are pretty limited. Exams are also tough but his homeworks and labs give you good practice for them. Most people probably don't actually do the homework but if you want extra practice then you should do it. He also gives opportunities for extra credit as mentioned in other reviews. I think there is no better professor you can take for CMSC216 and with Kauffman you'll be in good hands.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A
Anonymous
05/23/2025 		I took Kauffman in Fall 2024, but I'm just writing my review now. He's a nice guy and is very knowledgeable about what he's teaching, but I'm gonna be honest people are glazing him way too much. First of all, he speaks very technically in class with heavy technical jargon and doesn’t break down new concepts in a digestible way, which makes it hard to follow. Additionally, his slides have so much content on them that it's hard to digest and understand what exactly you're supposed to look at. The good thing though, is that he gives lots of extra credit, which is pretty rare to see in a CS class, so that’s nice. Another thing is, no offense to him, but he seems a little socially awkward. I talked to him in office hours and after class, and he seems to have a hard time speaking at a normal speed and has trouble making eye contact (another thing I’ve noticed with a lot of CS professors at UMD—a lot of them seem autistic or socially awkward). Another thing is that he has a lot of trouble taking criticism. In his midterm survey, so many students pointed out that there is a big difference in the level of difficulty between his practice and real exams, but he claims that because he has way more experience and is smarter than us, he knows a lot more than we do and dismisses literally any criticism. Overall, you will get a good grade in this class if you put in the effort, but him as a professor is not the best choice.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

05/20/2025 		It's clear that the following three reviews were all written by the same disgruntled student. Please take these with a grain of salt and do your own research before making any decisions. DO NOT take this class as it will ruin your emotional stability and cause immense paranoia like it did to me. Stay away from this man and protect yourself at ALL COSTS !!!!! >> very difficult and unhelpful lectures >> I don't think I understood much from Kauffman's lectures. His slides and lecture code was often hard to follow and he information overloads on his project docs, slides, and lab/hmw directions. Although some may find it helpful with given information, I often had a hard time understanding what exactly I am supposed to do with the given information and how I can learn/complete the tasks. I found his projects to be mentally breaking, and I don't agree that this is what is best for students. I believe this class took a huge toll on my mental health and emotional stability. but I don't think Kauffman's 216 would be a great learning experience as you would have to struggle for basically nothing. Last but not least, I don't even understand why we had to learn Assembly in his class because I find Assembly to be completely useless and no one uses it anymore in modern programming. Take this class with another professor if you can

05/17/2025 		Idk why his rating is so low tbh. Larry is super fair with the stuff he teaches if you go to lecture and actually pay attention you will be perfectly prepared for the exams. His projects are great because the descriptions get real specific about what you should do while also leaving some room for you to do it your way. 10 total points were free points of worksheets graded on completion that you just had to go to some lectures/discussions to complete. On top of this he curves. Getting an A in this class is easy if you regularly attend lecture and are half decent at programming.

CMSC216

Expecting an A
Anonymous
05/17/2025 		Kauffman and I had our enemies to lovers arc this semester. At the beginning of the semester with the stuff introducing C, I felt he went too slow, I felt the labs were unhelpful, and I felt bored in the class. But once Kauffman got to Assembly, and especially once he got to the UNIX and hardware material at the end of the semester, he really opened my eyes to learning about computer systems in general. Professor Kauffman has an enthusiastic lecturing style---you can tell he cares a lot about our understanding of the material, and the labs and homeworks he provides really do give great practice for his exams. His exams are also challenging, but definitely fair, and the open resources were a nice touch that I genuinely used, albeit barely, but when I used it it was absolutely needed. There was never a question I did not know how to do on his exams given the practice material provides you with exact question types you might see. Professor Kauffman has a plethora of pop culture references as well which keep him feeling simultaneously like your old uncle and someone in the know. I would recommend anyone take Professor Kauffman if you value your learning and your education more than your grade. His projects are difficult, but manageable if you start early, and I never had problems submitting on time nor with the actual coding itself. If you put in the work, you will get the A you desire. I have to dock one star because he called my supreme overlord Larry Herman "Professor" Herman. Everyone knows the GOAT is an instructor, NOT a professor.
Larry Herman
CMSC216

Expecting an A
Anonymous
05/17/2025 		Seeing a lot of people downvote Larry just because he's one of the first CS professors that they have at UMD. Larry will literally baby and hold your hand throughout this whole course- he does this by giving discussion worksheets where problems are literally pulled for the exam, LONG practice material documents with questions the exam same format as the exam, and LONG LONG LONG project descriptions that highlight practically every single edge case that you ever need to consider. The worksheets are literally just free padding to your grade (just show up!), so I'm not sure why people complain about that. They're literally not even graded for correctness, it's just like do 3/4 of the worksheet and boom instant 10/10. Larry tells you every single thing that you need to know and even how to know it (like just reading chapter X in the specified textbook), and the people complaining here are just the ones too lazy to do it.
Larry Herman
CMSC216

Expecting a B-

05/14/2025 		Do not take his class. Extremely boring lecturer who goes incredibly in depth on the topics and then expects you to pull out the most random piece of knowledge for his exams. His exams are also incredibly difficult, and he will punish you for not knowing the material by taking off additional points if you get some questions wrong. He then has the audacity to be surprised at why the class average is so low. You also can't skip class because there is a slight possibility of a worksheet in class, which is ultimately useless if you haven't been keeping up in the first place. The most I ever learned from this class was by doing the projects (they did teach me a lot about programming in C). Still, he also makes them incredibly over the top, and his directions are unclear, including unnecessary sentences, detracting from the main requirements or criteria you need to follow. That being said, his projects were generally easy to complete or learn to complete.

Expecting a B+
Anonymous
04/28/2025 		i had yoon for both 132 and 216, i feel like people who usually give reviews are losers but i have a hot take about yoon, honestly class isn't that hard, ik a lot of people do bad, but if you think you're semi smart you should be fine, both 132 and 216 i would learn the whole material like day before test and do fine, projects are usually not bad, every now and then there's a hard one, but its usually easy, 132 was genuinely not that bad, yoon is known for asking a lot of specific details, but for 132 as long as u get decent indepth understanding of the data structures and assumptions behind them, its not that bad, 216 midterms were honestly kinda hard cus c is known for having a lot of weird nuances abt it, but usually the coding problems were p easy, my hot take is yoon is a terrible teacher but people somewhat like him cus he seems like a nice guy but he isn't actually that nice, he's pretty rigid with the rules, in my experience he doesn't give late extensions unless you have a doctors note before, i feel like most people wouldnt think its that bad but i think its kinda crazy imagine ur dying sick like ur teacher really gonna make you get out of bed and get a doctors note from across the campus and a sometimes you can't even get doctors note one 1 day notice, honestly i didnt care abt this until i realized all the other teachers usually give u the benefit of the doubt, atp i feel like yoon doesnt have too much going for him, hes probably a nice guy tbh, but he doesn't really give u any slack, overall id say very rigid with rules, doesn't teach very well, not that hard curriculum, good lecture slides and easy to self study usually, and not too hard to get like a b range grade, kinda hard to get an a though midterms were kinda easy, final for 216 was hard assembly projects were easy, i lowk had a very surface level understanding of assembly so i didn't do great in final, but overall id say very doable class, also ik a lot of people say its the most difficult class, idk if id disagree with that statement, but i really dont think it was that bad, like i wouldnt say i put in that much effort and i did relatively fine
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting a B
Anonymous
04/17/2025 		Now keep in mind, it's 216, so the class isn't already easy. Concepts are already harder than usual when compared to content from 131/132. So I wouldn't say it's really easy to get an A in 216 generally. Pros: - Teaches really well. Like really well. You will understand computer systems on a deeper level than any other professor teaching 216 in my opinion. - He loves teaching. That means he's not like one of those professors with really bad quality slides and unorganized course structure. The entire course is structured and organized really well under him, so you'll definitely enjoy 216 somewhat with him. - Pre-recorded lectures. That means no need to go to lecture and you should still do fine - Lots of extra credit opportunities. Basically go to his classes, participate, and get engagement points, which can help you get extra credits. Even by participating on Piazza by responding to other people's questions should give you engagement points as well. He also gives these weekly quizzes which is basically like a free grade to you since you get unlimited tries. - Open Notes exams. This is honestly the best part of the class. Take really good notes in my opinion, organize it all really well (I suggest using Obsidian notes since that's what I used to organize it all). This is also why his exams are generally harder than usual I believe. - No secret tests on projects. This is honestly the best part of the projects. It would be horrible if there were secret tests as it would take eons to debug them. Cons: - Hard projects. Yea I had trouble doing his projects at times since it's quite convoluted and wack and sometimes really long. Especially the Assembly project (all the best with that one). It's not bad as in impossible Yoon level bad, it's just quite challenging and you will be getting a lot of headaches by trying to figure out his projects, but they do generally help with understanding the material. - Hard exams. Compared to Nelson, Kauffman's exams are generally hard. So you will have to study and practice a lot for his exams, as well as his final. It's not like impossible hard, it's do able, but there aren't a lot of practice materials in my opinion so yeah you'll have to look at his recordings, projects, and other materials for practice and all. REMEMBER, study your projects really well, it's definitely almost always on all of his exams. Overall he's a great professor, but I suggest taking Nelson if you can (although he can have some cons too) as he provides more exam materials.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A
Anonymous
04/16/2025 		Overall a pretty good lecturer. Class is usually engaging, but sometimes he takes a lot of questions which can make the class feel a bit boring at times. I stopped going to discussions after the first few because they're optional, but you should go if you need help with the weekly labs. The weekly homeworks are easy to get 100% on since you can keep resubmitting each MCQ question until you get it right (although you should probably try to do it seriously). The projects are very lengthy, so make sure to start them ASAP. Exams are open note, but they are all conceptual questions, so you don't really look at your notes all that often. The exams are also pretty long and take almost the full 75 minutes allotted. There is a ton of opportunity for extra credit through completing all the labs (you only need to do 10 of them for full credit, but there are 14 total), asking questions during lecture, and answering piazza posts.
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Expecting an A
Anonymous
02/20/2025 		Extremely engaging lecturer and will make sure you remember concepts. Frequently reviews material and common misconceptions. Will not let anyone use their laptops during class, which is great since I always get distracted when nobody else pays attention and the vibe is distracted. Bad if you need to take notes on it, but honestly taking notes is useless in this class since A) he posts everything on the slides online B) he really makes sure you remember stuff by explaining things many times. His projects are actually fun and he ACTUALLY PROVIDES material on how to use grace and unix unlike some teachers. Every concept has a resource and detailed explanation online. He does password protect his project pdfs, which I found annoying at first but appreciated since it made me have to type out commands for a few seconds and thus learn what to do. Has graded assignments in class but based on completion and guaranteed free points as long as you show up. The assignments really help since he goes over it and its much more easy to remember than just listening for hours. Participation is also free points as long as you ask a question or answer a question.
Christopher Kauffman

02/02/2025 		It's all been said already, but he's amazing. However, don't mistake the high rating as meaning that he gives out free A's. If you want that, take Nelson. Anonymous
01/29/2025 		I want to start by saying that this course is hard. This was personally my first time having to go through learning about systems in depth and using some of the most barebone languages in coding. But Prof Kauffman is one of the best lecturers I have ever had. He is very understanding and communicated great with us students. Assignments such as quizzes and labs are the easiest grade boosts. The organization of the class with all of the labs, projects, and his websites were very useful. I rarely was engaged with lectures, but the recordings and slides were very detailed and easy to catch up with. In terms of the exams, I believe they were very fair. Along with being open note, they coincided heavily with his lecture examples and practice exam, but most with the labs and projects associated with the class. As long as you put in just a little effort for the things that matter like exams and projects, you should pass this class perfectly fine.

CMSC216

Expecting a B
Anonymous
01/25/2025 		Professor Yoon is a laid-back guy, but not the best teacher. He tends to go off on long tangents that don't add much value and mostly teaches directly from the slides, which can feel pretty slow and unengaging. The practice exams and projects he provides don’t really align with the actual tests, which can be frustrating. On the bright side, the test averages are usually in the 60s, so there’s a generous curve. I ended with a 74% and got a B so if that's the kind of thing you're into this is the guy for you.
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Expecting an A+
Anonymous
01/19/2025 		To whom it may concern, Yoon's exams can be challenging. To do well on them, make sure you really play around with edge cases on the compiler, modify the lecture example code. Do not think about this class as a class about C, just how bits and such are set. Assembly will inevitably feel hard, just practice and it should be ok. For projects, many of the hidden test cases are decently predictable, make sure your program can handle what the spec says it should handle. For example, if you are making a playlist program and the spec says the number of songs will never exceed 80, make sure you test how the program handles an empty playlist or a completely full one. They can really take a chunk out of your grade, but these hidden tests are usually just testing small edge cases. Make sure you check gradescope for the lab assignments as they aren't always announced. He sets cuttoffs at the end, I think they were lowered by around 3% for my specific scenario. He is a really nice guy.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting a B
Anonymous
01/06/2025 		I wasn’t a huge fan of this class overall. The projects were challenging, but they were also very effective in helping me understand the concepts covered in lectures and I liked that they were all public tests. My biggest tip for future students is to focus on fully understanding the projects and not just finishing them, as this will help a lot on exams. Many exam questions required implementing a function as an extension of a recent project, which I found a bit unfair. Projects are designed to take time and allow collaboration with TAs, so having similar tasks on timed exams felt unrealistic. On the other hand, the homeworks and labs were helpful for reinforcing concepts. However, one thing I didn’t like was having to complete them before those concepts were taught in lecture a lot of the time. This often made them feel pointless initially, and I ended up revisiting the labs and homeworks closer to exams to actually learn from them. What I struggled with most were the exams. Despite being open note, they were extremely difficult, and the single practice exam provided by Kauffman was significantly easier than the actual exams, making it less useful for preparation. Even though I reviewed all the labs, homeworks, projects, lecture slides, and attended or watched every lecture, I still found the exams to be tough.
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Expecting an A+
Anonymous
01/06/2025 		Anyone who got below an A in this class was clearly either not trying or not paying any attention. He gives the answers to the exam during his lecture, you just have to listen.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A-
Anonymous
01/05/2025 		Very solid professor, so much better than the other 216 professors. Kauffman is a great lecturer and makes concepts easy to understand, but whenever a student asks a question, he happens to go on a 5-15 minute tangent answering that question and makes lecture boring and hard to pay attention to. Despite this, his slides and lectures are really useful (he also records his lectures so it is not mandatory to go to lecture). Additionally, discussion depends on your TAs but im my opinion, it is useless. Most student havent gone since the first week or so of class and have made it out fine. My tas barely taught for like 5 mins and thats it. one thing to keep in mind is: START PROJECTS EARLY (AND DO NOT CHATGPT THEM). Kauffman tends to make very lengthy projects and even asks project related questions (even coding questions) on his exam, so make sure to lock in for those. Exams, on the other hand, weren't that bad tbh. Take your time and do not rush them because they will mostly take the whole time. Just because it is open note doesnt mean that you should not study at all because open note is not that useful (still useful tho).
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Expecting an A-
Anonymous
12/29/2024 		Projects are the same as Nelsons but so are the practice tests which are NOTHING like the actual tests. The average for every test is low to mid 60s I stayed a few points above average for every test and made sure to get as many points as possible through labs, excercises, and projects. I've never had test anxiety before this class but this class made me get it because no matter how much you study the tests are awful. The amount of crashouts I had over exams is insane but looking back if you have to take this professor you will be ok as long as you focus on projects, labs, and excercises. Sometimes he posts labs without announcing it so if youre not in class you could miss it so make sure to stay on top of that. Yoon was nice when I had to makeup a quiz hes a good guy but if youre class is averaging 60s for exams its not every students fault, its the professors and he needs to realize that. When I took 216 the options were Nelson, Kauffman, Yoon but no spaces for Nelson or Kauffman were open bbut if I couldve picked based on my friends experiences I would pick Nelson.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A-
Anonymous
12/29/2024 		Kauffman is an amazing CS professor. I would 100% recommend taking him. That being said, here is how his class works: Lectures are 75-minutes, he records everything, but it is still helpful to attend since he is a good lecturer and he takes a lot of questions during lecture. Each time you ask a question, it counts as extra credit. Discussions are pretty much free. We are required to complete at least 10 labs for the course (there are 12 labs in total). The TA will pretty much give the answers to the lab in discussion and you're allowed to collaborate with others on it. On top of that, close to project deadlines, discussions are converted to extra office hours which can be pretty helpful. It is really easy to get a 100% on every homework since it gives you the option to submit each question and the entire form. After every question, it tells you whether or not your answer was right, and you can keep resubmitting until you get all questions right. The projects are very long and difficult. Start early cause office hours (located on the 4th floor of A.V. Williams for some reason) fills up very fast and it can be either very hard or not possible to get help around the project deadline. In terms of difficulty, I found projects 1, 3, and 5 to be the most time consuming. Project 3 was both the most difficult and time consuming since it was the assembly project. The late penalty is very lenient, so there not much of problem with turning them in late. The exams are open note, but they can be quite hard. Rather than focusing on memorizing random things, they try to test big picture concepts and your ability to understand and apply them to sample programs given. There is no multiple choice, only short answer and coding questions. Furthermore, the exams can be a time crunch since almost all questions need a detailed response, so it's very easy to run out of time if you don't manage it on the exam. Despite how hard the projects and exams can be I still recommend taking Kauffman since he is a great teacher, a really nice and helpful person, and you will learn a lot of useful concepts from his class.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A
Anonymous
12/26/2024 		Great professor to take 216 with if you want to learn something. Good lecturer, fair exams (if you study and considering that they're open note), and extra credit on projects/exams/lectures. 216 is a hard class at Maryland but Kauffman is probably your best shot for learning. Take Nelson if you want an easy but less in depth class.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A+
Anonymous
12/25/2024 		So much extra credit. Discussions are useless. Lectures are engaging. You can easily excel in the class even without touching the textbook. If you go to (or watch recordings of) all the lectures and complete all labs and homework on your own time honestly (not just copying or clicking through) it's a surprisingly easy A.
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Expecting an A-
Anonymous
12/25/2024 		This class with this professor is designed to weed out students that are not serious about CS. I believe because I got an A- (I did not attend to any TA office hours or any office hours for this class because idk) there is definitely a very good possibility for you to understand and pass this class with yoon. I cannot defend his exams because it is a curveball, he thinks they're are fair in his eyes because he assumes you go to every lecture, understand everything, attend office hours, and attend every discussion.... but that's not reality. I am an average student and am not smart by any means, all I did was spend hours on the content he would teach, and spend time on it until it made sense and I can replicate his example codes (that's essentially what the projects are). The only way through this class is TIME and be WILLING to lose a good amount of sleep. Spend enough time on anything and you can learn anything in this class. That's a promise. Jesus loves you!
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Anonymous
12/24/2024 		He's clearly a very knowledgeable professor. Additionally, he's an approachable and is more than happy to clarify concepts that you need clarification for. So overall, as a person and instructor he's great. The only gripe are how he gives assessments. He does not prepare you at all for exams, and he will sometimes add questions relating to one very esoteric or specific thing that he talked about once during lecture and not present in any lecture slides, lab assignments, and projects. Additionally, he'll sometimes give past exams or worksheets, but these also do not reflect the type of questions in exams. So, this is what makes his exams a lot more challenging. You will do fine in the class as long as you're not doing unrelated stuff on your laptop or phone like playing brawl stars or doom scrolling. Pay attention during lecture, take and REVIEW notes, and you will be fine. I believe he gives a reasonable curve at the end, I think a 75 is a B.
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Expecting a C
Anonymous
12/24/2024 		If you are reading this to decide what professor to take and you are early enough not to pick Yoon, dont. However, if you have to take him do not spend the months leading up to the class worrying like I did. Yoon’s class is not equivalent to all of these reviews and you need to go in which a much more positive mindset than these reviews let on. It is definitely true that my friends in Nelson and Kauffman had an easier time and arguably learned more, however having Yoon does not mean that you are going to fail. I am no where near a genius, in fact I was just hoping to pass 216 knowing how hard would be, especially with Yoon and I ended with a B. What is takes to do well in Yoon’s course is hard work. His exams are impossible and not fair. The test material does not reflect what’s taught in class and labs so I would say that doing bad on exams is expected (class average was never higher than a 66). Quizzes also very hard. The projects are doable, start early. The lab assignments are free As if you attend lab. You need to try very hard to do well on projects and lab assignments because these free points will average out exam grades. More importantly, you need to take advantage of Yoon’s willingness to help. Ive seen other reviews say this and it is so true.. Yoon is truly SUCH a nice guy. I had a family emergency and Yoon granted me an extension and offered to help fix my code without even considering it for a second. I went to Yoon’s personal office hours multiple time throughout the semester to ask questions from lecture or go over old exam problems, and I learned a ton. You will excel if you build a relationship with Yoon, and I suggest starting early!! The class was averaging low scores, so towards the end of the semester Yoon assigned an easy extra credit assignment that would add points to your final grade and yes, Yoon curved our final grades. In conclusion, you can absolutely do well in this class without being a genius, Yoon just does not make it easy. You need to attend every lab and lecture, earning as many free points as you can and studying enough to stay with the average exam score. Most importantly, review lecture slides and if you are ever confused (or just want Yoon to know who you are), GO SEE HIM!!!
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Expecting a B
Anonymous
12/24/2024 		This class was an absolute hell scape. Final grades just got posted this morning, Christmas Eve, when the final was on December 12th. I guess he was grading his final project 6 grades as well - which had a due date of December 20th???? My final grade was a 76, which between about 3 extra credit points (which he said applied post curve?) got me to a B. I learned way more watching YouTube videos and googling the content on the lecture slides than I did going to this mans lectures. I still went to lectures, unlike 50% of the class, because his Panopto recordings are basically useless otherwise. This man LOVES his chalkboard - and none of it gets picked up by Panopto. It gets better. No one in the back 60% of the class can read the chalkboard either. His handwriting kinda sucks, but its also just small? As a back of the class sitter, I had to go to the front because I couldn't read not only the chalkboard, but also the code on the screen. He also likes to mumble. Also, god forbid you have a yapper in the class... dude has no self control and will have an entire conversation with one student about the most niche topic thats not even remotely related to the lecture. As for discussions, no one goes (unless you have a really good TA, which most don't). They teach you things in discussions at the beginning of the year that they don't teach in lecture. That's where we learned gdb commands and stuff about valgrind and splint. You're also supposed to go over memory maps in discussion, but my TA was running behind so we never got to it, and lo and behold I did bad on the memory map questions :'(. Either way, these things show up on exams, and aren't taught in lecture; but, they are all recorded, so you can watch yours *or* your favorite TA's recordings. Honestly, I didn't think the projects were awful. That was actually where I did best in the class. The worst thing about them is the like week and a half deadline... combined with my awful schedule I was often pulling an all-nighter a day or two before it was due. The project I did worst on was when I was like deadly sick for a week and then just didn't finish it well. And then the next project relied on it lol. I usually lost a few points on them, but not a ton. This is 35% of your grade so it's important to focus on these. The exams are the real reason everyone hates this man. 50% of your grade is exams (actually lower than most coding classes from what I've seen, but still a lot). He will post "practices" like 3 days before exams and quizzes, saying they are supposed to help you. Sometimes the content on them is literally not on the exam/quiz AT ALL??? He likes to do this to waste your time :). What you actually need to be doing is studying the most obscure edge case you've never been taught, a bunch of random linux and gdb commands you were never taught, and just like 3 or 4 pretty hard coding questions. All that studying only for class averages of 67, 66, and 65 for the midterms and final, respectively. Me personally, I genuinely didn't have enough time to study well, so I did like 5-10% worse than average on the midterms, and then did almost 10% better on the final. For the quizzes, we had means of 62 and 66! The only thing he does to help boost your grade is the coding exercises and lab work. There were 10 lab assignments and 4 exercises, with a fifth exercise for 2% extra credit. These are generally easy and are about 15% of your grade. However, if you miss the announcement that they get posted, you might lose points for turning in late/not at all. tl:dr, if you are a normal student, the grades will be super low, and you will likely be super stressed. While Yoon is genuinely a nice person, he is a very mean professor. You will be stressed for both exams and projects. Make sure you teach yourself the content, and try your best not to fall behind. If you didn't read the grade data posted above - 17% of people fail this class, and like 8% withdraw. If all the registration spots close for every other professor like they did for me, I am very sorry, and good luck.
Ilchul Yoon
CMSC216

Expecting a C
Anonymous
12/24/2024 		If you are reading this in order to decide what professor to take and you are early enough to not pick Yoon, don't. However, do not stress for the months leading up to the course if you have to take him like I did. His course is not equivalent to all of these reviews and you should go in with a more positive mindset than these reviews let on. I am not a genius, in fact I was just hoping to pass 216 knowing how hard it would be, especially with Yoon. Without a doubt, my friends in Nelson and Kauffman had a much easier time and arguably learned more. However, Yoon does not mean you are failing the class, I ended with a B. What it takes to do well in this class is hard work. His exams are impossible and not fair. They do not resemble the material taught in class, so doing bad on the exams I would say is expected (class average was no higher than a 66). The projects are totally doable, start early. The lab assignments are also free As if you attend labs. I did not miss a single lecture or lab. I tried very hard on projects and lab assignments trying to get as many "free" points as I could which will balance out the exam grades. What it comes down to as well is that Yoon is truly such a nice guy. I had an emergency and he granted me an extension without even considering it. You have to take advantage of his willingness to help. I went to Yoon's personal office hours multiple times to ask questions from lectures or go over exam questions. During these sessions I learned a ton. You should try very hard to build a relationship with Yoon and start this early in the semester. While the class was averaging low scores, he gave us an easy extra credit assignment towards the end of the semester and yes at the end of the course, he did curve our final grades. In conclusion, I wouldnt be eager to take his classes and he makes it very difficult but trying to get as many points as you can and studying enough to stay with the average on exams will get you very far. Most importantly, review the lecture slides after class and if you are confused GO SEE HIM!!!
Christopher Kauffman

12/18/2024 		I personally was not a big fan of this class. Kauffman is a great lecturer and makes concepts easy to understand, but there were some elements of the class that I was not a big fan of. Expecting a B
Anonymous
12/18/2024 		Kauffman is the GOAT. Best teaching style I have ever come across. 216 is a hard class but he made the concepts very understandable. All exams are open note which is helpful at times but does not help that much, you still have to know what you are doing. My only issue with him is that I think the projects are very very difficult, sometimes too difficult imo. The projects do help you prepare for the exams tho. Overall very good prof, very good speaker, very good at breaking down hard cs concepts. Although I got a worse grade in 216 then 132 I feel like I understand the course material way better. Ending note Kaufman is the GOAT and an absolute gift to the CS department(Just please make the projects easier)

CMSC216

Expecting a B+
Anonymous
12/18/2024 		No professor is perfect but Prof K comes as close to that as one can expect. His lectures are well planned and the course material is as well. The labs are well planned out and give you an introduction to the material. Make sure do them yourself and understand what going on same. Same thing can be said with the weekly homeworks that have quizzes (you can retake until you get a 100%) but make sure to understand what you are doing. Make sure to understand the code pack as it's impossible to go through everything in lecture and fully understand it. His projects are the hardest I had so far in CS here at UMD. The take hours to complete and much more complicated and intricate than projects you have done in 131/132. Though there are only 5 of them, each take hours to complete which each becoming progressively harder, but not neccesarily longer. While in 131/132 you could do projects that day off or a night before you CAN'T do that here. His projects take a minimum of 10-15 hours and the assembly one took me upwards of 25-30 hours to complete. Furthermore his exams are not as easy as the appear. The practice material is standardized and very meek with some resemblance but little to none. Exam 1 I found was hard but Exam 2 was easier though assembly is a much harder concept. The open resource policy can be deceiving but Kauffman does not ask theory questions on his exams as much, given the open resource. Make sure to plan resourceful notes that have tons of examples regarding coding and debugging and analyzing issues. Kauffman belief is that you should have access to your laptop and is a very takes an industry approach to the class. His grading structure put an heavy emphasis on exams each being worth 20% and the final the same (60% total). Other professors had much laxer exam percentages and focused on projects and lab exercises more. His labs quizzes are grade boosters as you can a 100%. I would have wished for a 3rd exam and each exam being worth like 12-15% and the projects being worth more as I spent hours on them. TLDR: Harder teacher than most, if you want an easier teacher who's funny take Nelson but if you really want to understand Operating Systems take Kauffman.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A
Anonymous
12/16/2024 		Kauffman is the goat!! I'd highly recommend him for 216! It's a difficult class but he's a good lecturer & has a fun personality. Lots of opportunities for extra credit (not just from participating in lecture). Discussions are not required, I did not go to a single one. Exams are open note, open lecture slides, open past coding projects, etc. Exams are graded leniently too. No multiple choice on exams (which I consider a good thing). There are 5 projects over the course of the semester. This is a lot fewer than in 131/132, but they are much more challenging. That said, the projects felt very applicable to real life and it felt like you'd gained useful skills once you completed one (except for the Assembly one maybe). Start the projects early. Office hours are packed, use Piazza instead and read other people's posts to avoid issues before you encounter them.
Christopher Kauffman
CMSC216

Expecting an A
Anonymous
12/15/2024 		Solid professor. His lectures are reasonably interesting and they explain the content well. His discussions are honestly completely useless but they operate as extra office hours later in the semester so if you need extra help with a project or want greater understanding of a lab you can go. He gives labs & "quizzes" which serve as grade boosters and learning checks (I say "quizzes" because you can retake until 100%) His exams are very fair and are open note. This forces you to prepare notes for the exam because you will need to reference them at times but by creating notes you are also studying so I like the format. The slides have alot of info and all lectures are recorded so you could not show up if you want (i did but you do you). Overall solid teacher, recorded lectures, optional discussion, fair open note exams.

12/11/2024 		First of all, this professor's lectures do not help to clarify or convey important information needed for projects and exams. Often times, his lectures start off with the concept but devolve into other unimportant topics that do not apply to projects. I find myself learning the material on my own. Second, his exams are not fair. As in, they test less on your general understanding of the subject, and more on "edge cases" or scenarios that the average student is unlikely to run into during their time with the programming language. Both the first and second midterms for this class had an average score of 66/100. With the average of the class just on the edge of a C- Students who pay attention to the class and students who get a good exam grade are not correlated for this professor in any way. Avoid him if you can. If you do HAVE to take him (due to sections for other professors being filled up), try your best to learn on your own and hopefully with a good TA. It will be the only way to make it through his course with a good grade.

11/25/2024 		Very generous and kind professor who cares about his students and what they learn. The material in 216 is somewhat difficult but Kauffman does his best to simplify it and make it easy to digest. He is very lenient with students, willing to push due dates and deadlines back when necessary. His tests are open note but not necessarily easy, so while notes are helpful it's important that you understand core concepts. Projects get progressively more difficult as the semester goes on but there are plenty of resources from office hours almost every day to relevant piazza posts. Kauffman is always in a happy mood and keeps class very engaging and offers extra credit when you participate in class. Overall an amazing professor and I would recommend taking his class.

10/22/2024 		Very messy professor, he will gives you deadline after deadline as if his class is the only class that matters, as if you are not studying any other classes.
